title: Emergency Care: Cat Dragging Hind Legs (Hind Limb Paralysis)
meta_desc: If your cat is dragging its hind legs, this is a life-threatening medical emergency. Learn about saddle thrombus and why you need a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden hind limb paralysis in cats is a life-threatening emergency, often caused by a blood clot, requiring immediate 24-hour veterinary care. If your cat cannot move its back legs and shows signs of pain, transport them to a hospital right away.

What causes sudden hind limb paralysis in my cat?

When a cat suddenly loses the ability to move its back legs, it is usually a sign of hind limb paralysis. The most common cause is Feline Aortic Thromboembolism (FATE), also known as a saddle thrombus. This occurs when a blood clot, often originating from an underlying heart condition, travels through the aorta and lodges where the arteries branch to the hind legs, cutting off all blood circulation. Other causes can include spinal cord injuries, severe disk herniation, or neurological trauma.

Is my cat's inability to move its back legs a veterinary emergency?

  • Immediate action required: This is a high-priority, life-threatening emergency.
  • Seek care now: Do not wait for an appointment; transport your cat to the nearest 24-hour emergency veterinary hospital immediately.
  • Pain management: If your cat is dragging its legs, it is likely experiencing extreme pain and physical distress.
  • Recognize critical signs: Symptoms such as vocalizing (crying out), heavy breathing, and cold-to-the-touch back paws indicate a critical loss of blood flow that requires instant intervention.

How can a photo or video help the vet triage my cat's condition?

  • Provide visual context: If it is safe to do so without delaying your departure, a quick video of your cat attempting to move can be invaluable for the veterinary team.
  • Identify the type of mobility loss: A video allows the vet to see whether the issue is complete paralysis, extreme weakness, or a lack of coordination (ataxia).
  • Speed up treatment: This helps the clinical team prepare the necessary diagnostic tools and pain management protocols before you even arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Posterior paresis or paralysis in cats can manifest as the inability to move the hindlimbs, while the cat can often move the legs above the stifles; the tail is commonly unaffected, and in some cases, only one hindlimb is affected. Diagnosis is commonly based on clinical signs, physical examination findings, and Doppler blood flow readings of the hindlimbs. Ultrasound can also be used to identify thromboemboli. Pathologic changes are present distal to the level of the middle to lower thigh and are characterized as focal muscle necrosis and degeneration of the central portions of the sciatic nerve and its branches. Management consists of analgesics, nursing care, and treatment of any underlying disease. Anticoagulants, such as unfractionated heparin or low-molecular-weight heparin, are used to reduce continued thrombus formation. Neurologic deficits may improve within 2-3 weeks, but 6 months may be required for complete recovery, and permanent deficits are possible.

Chapter: Cardiology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1245)

faqs.question: Is hind limb paralysis painful for cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, especially if caused by a blood clot (saddle thrombus), this condition is considered one of the most painful experiences a cat can have. Immediate pain management is a top priority for vets.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a blood clot in a cat's legs?
faqs.answer: Common signs include sudden paralysis, loud crying or howling, cold back paws, and paw pads that look blue or pale instead of pink.

faqs.question: Can a cat recover after dragging its back legs?
faqs.answer: Recovery depends entirely on the underlying cause. While some spinal issues may respond to surgery or rest, a saddle thrombus has a very guarded prognosis and requires intensive emergency care.
